interactions.summary: A student connects school to degrees and work; Jiang answers that school serves the nation-state by making people obey, fight, and die for it.

Jiang closes by naming money, the individual, and the nation-state as concepts beyond human experience that monotheism created, then defines power as alchemy: the capacity to turn nothing into everything and possibly to create a new system for eudaimonia.
A brief 'Okay?' fragment is diarized as unknown and carries no substantive content.

speaker_notes.note: Student wording is noisy; the response appears to link schooling to degrees and work.

claims.claim: The point of school is to serve the nation-state by making people obey, serve, fight, and die for it.
Money, the individual, and the nation-state are concepts beyond ordinary human experience, requiring brainwashing to believe.
Jiang says monotheism is the revolution in human thought that produced money, the individual, and the nation-state and thereby created the modern world.
Power is alchemy: the capacity to turn nothing into everything by making people believe money is valuable, the individual can produce happiness, and the nation-state exists.
The present system was an accident of human imagination, and because imagination made it, imagination can create a new system oriented toward eudaimonia and intellectual flourishing.

signature_moments.moment: A visitor from a thousand years ago would see modern concepts as slavery.
signature_moments.source_phrase: This means that you're all slaves.
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It dramatizes the distance between Jiang's premodern human baseline and modern abstractions.

signature_moments.moment: Power is alchemy because it turns belief in nothing into the whole lived world.
signature_moments.source_phrase: Power is a capacity to turn nothing into everything.
signature_moments.why_it_matters: This is the final compressed definition of the lecture.

signature_moments.moment: The system was accidental, so imagination can make another one.
signature_moments.source_phrase: It's an accident of the human imagination.
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It keeps the episode from ending in fatalism and returns to the opening Kantian method.

glossary_terms.definition_or_usage: Jiang's definition of power as turning nothing into everything by making imagined concepts socially real.
The one-true-God revolution in human thought that Jiang says created money, individual, nation-state, and the modern world.

chronology_notes.note: This lecture sets up later classes on Judaism, Christianity, Islam, and monotheism; it is a course-opening thesis rather than the fully developed later argument.

uncertainty_notes.note: Jiang says the three great monotheistic religions are 'basically the same religion'; public read should present this as a promised later argument, not expand it without later-source support.
